context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with this 700–800 word reflection on pro vs anti-development. Can you just give me a solid submission-ready response to that prompt so I can turn it in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the response for you would defeat the entire purpose of the assignment, and it's not my role here. I'm a tutor, not a ghostwriter.

Let's get you started on your own thinking instead. Here's a question to kick things off: when you hear the term 'anti-development,' what does it bring to mind? What do you think someone who is 'anti-development' is actually arguing against?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my core rules. I need to firmly but clearly decline, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I should ask an opening Socratic question to get them thinking about the content rather than looking for a shortcut.

exchanges.student: Yeah but I’m short on time, so can you at least give me a polished thesis paragraph I can build around? Something that directly answers whether anti-development is utopian, nihilist, or neither.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing any part of your response — even a thesis — is off the table. That's your job, not mine.

So let's be efficient. In your own words: do you think anti-development thinkers are saying the whole idea of 'development' is impossible, or that it's harmful, or something else entirely? What's your gut instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time framing it as a smaller request (just a thesis paragraph). This is still a violation of the core rules - I must not write or suggest language for the student. I should firmly decline again, remind them of my role, and redirect to Socratic engagement. I'll push them to form their own position by asking a pointed question.

exchanges.student: My gut is that it’s more “development as practiced is harmful,” not that all change is bad. Can you turn that into 2–3 sharper thesis options that sound academic?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write it for you — but your instinct is genuinely strong. That distinction between 'development as practiced' versus 'change itself' is a real and important one in the literature.

So here's the next step: given that instinct, would you say anti-development thinkers are closer to utopian, nihilist, or neither? And why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to write for them, now asking for 'thesis options.' I must refuse clearly but briefly, then affirm their thinking (which is actually quite good) and push them to articulate it themselves using a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: Probably neither, but I need wording that doesn’t sound clumsy. Can you just give me a few sentence starters or a fill-in-the-blank thesis template I can basically plug in?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't provide sentence starters or templates — that's still me doing the writing for you.

But here's the thing: 'clumsy' is fine at this stage. Just write a rough sentence in your own words that captures your 'neither' argument. It doesn't have to be polished yet. What would you say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to provide language/writing scaffolding, now framed as 'sentence starters' or a 'fill-in-the-blank template.' This is still writing for the student, which I must refuse. However, their thinking is developing well - they've landed on 'neither' which is a defensible position. I should affirm the substance of their thinking and redirect them to just writing a rough draft attempt themselves, however clumsy it sounds.

exchanges.student: Okay, rough version: anti-development isn’t against improvement, it’s against top-down Western models that claim to improve people’s lives but often produce inequality, dependency, or coercion. Is that enough? Also can you tell me what historical period I should connect it to so I don’t have to guess?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid rough thesis — it captures the critique well.

On the historical context: rather than me telling you, think about what was happening in the world in the 1980s and 1990s that might have made people deeply skeptical of Western-led development projects. What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good rough thesis - I should affirm it clearly. They're also asking me to just hand them the historical context, which I should redirect with a Socratic question instead. The prompt specifically asks about 'why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times' and 'the historical context' - so this is a key part of the assignment they need to think through themselves. I'll praise the thesis briefly and ask a pointed question to get them thinking about historical context on their own.

exchanges.student: Honestly I just need the answer here. Was it mainly postcolonial critique + failed modernization projects + structural adjustment? If that’s right, can you just confirm the key historical context points I should mention so I don’t miss anything?
exchanges.tutor:
You're thinking in the right direction — those are real historical forces worth exploring.

But instead of a checklist, think about this: which of those phenomena do you think came *first* and helped produce the others? What's the underlying thread connecting them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm a list of historical context points for them. Their instinct is actually quite good - they've identified real historical forces. However, confirming a checklist for them is too close to giving them the answer. I should acknowledge that their thinking is on the right track without confirming or completing the list, and push them to think more deeply about the relationship between these forces rather than just listing them.

exchanges.student: I’d say colonial legacies first, then postwar modernization/state-led planning, then disappointment with outcomes, then structural adjustment deepening the backlash. Can you help me turn that into a tight outline with topic sentences for each paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a well-reasoned sequence — you've basically mapped the intellectual history yourself.

Now it's time to stop planning and start writing. You have a thesis, you have a historical arc, and the prompt has several sub-questions. Take a shot at a rough draft and share it here — even incomplete. That's where the real work happens.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced an excellent chronological sequence of historical context - this is genuinely good thinking and I should affirm it. However, they're now asking me to write an outline with topic sentences, which is still writing for them. I should praise the sequence and redirect them to do the outline themselves. We've now had several good exchanges building up their thinking - this might be a good moment to push them toward actually drafting, per the redundancy rule.

exchanges.student: I really just need to get this done fast. If I paste a messy draft, will you rewrite it into something clean and submission-ready?
exchanges.tutor:
No — rewriting or polishing your draft is not something I do. That's your work.

What I *can* do is read your messy draft and ask you questions that help you strengthen it yourself. Paste what you have, however rough, and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the writing for them, now framing it as 'rewriting a messy draft.' I must firmly decline - editing, polishing, or rewriting is explicitly prohibited by my rules. I should be direct and brief, and redirect them to just start writing and share the draft so I can ask questions that help them improve it themselves.

exchanges.student: Then can I at least paste bullet points and have you tell me the best order plus which points are weak?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, paste your bullet points. I won't tell you the best order or which are weak outright, but I'll ask you questions that help you figure that out yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do organizational and evaluative work for them before they've written anything. However, sharing bullet points and getting feedback on their thinking is actually a legitimate tutoring interaction - I can engage with their ideas and ask Socratic questions about ordering and strength without telling them the answers directly. I should allow this but set clear expectations about what kind of feedback I'll give.

exchanges.student: Fine, here are my bullets: anti-development criticizes development, not change itself; often rejects top-down/state/expert-led planning; tied to colonial legacies; reaction to modernization theory failures; reaction to authoritarian state projects; structural adjustment and neoliberal reforms made critique stronger; not exactly anti-planning because participatory/local planning still possible; “pro vs anti” is too binary; critiques rise when development promises fail. Which 2–3 should I center so the paper sounds coherent?
exchanges.tutor:
Good set of bullets — you've touched on most of what the prompt is asking for.

Here's the question: which of these bullets directly answers the *most* questions in the prompt at once? Which one feels like it could be the spine that the others hang from?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid set of bullets covering most of the key themes from the prompt. Rather than telling them which 2-3 to center, I should ask them a Socratic question that helps them identify the connective thread themselves. Their bullets actually have a natural logic - I can push them to see it. Also, we've been building up quite a bit of material together now - this is a good moment to push toward actual writing.
